title: Urban food delivery services as extreme heat adaptation

data_scope.behavioral_signal: online food delivery order records (proprietary, NDA)
data_scope.weather_source: NOAA GSOD via R package GSODR

core_conclusion: Urban residents in China use food delivery as an immediate behavioral adaptation to extreme heat: lunchtime orders rise over 12.6% from 20C to 35C and 21.4% at 40C across 100 cities (2017-2023). Benefits accrue more to female, high-income and older consumers, while heat exposure is transferred from consumers to delivery riders, making the service both adaptation and inequity and calling for policy intervention.
why_it_matters: First large-scale quantification of a market-mediated, digital-service behavioral adaptation to heat; reveals a hidden consumer-to-rider exposure transfer; maps which demographic groups can afford to adapt; gives concrete levers for rider heat-protection and platform regulation.
why_nature: Couples city-scale weather to individual behavior and labor exposure; novel proprietary platform data across 100 cities and seven years; equity and environmental-justice framing; directly policy-translatable for climate adaptation and labor protection.
added_value_to_my_research: Street-level mapping of consumer->rider heat-exposure transfer via spatiotemporal KDE on LST/shade rasters
Invert order-elasticity into an affordability-weighted heat-adaptive accessibility (2SFCA/R2SFCA) index
Chain CMIP6 spread through elasticity with Monte-Carlo for rider labor-loss forecasting
Explainable ML (MGWR + SHAP) to attribute demographic/built-environment drivers of adaptive consumption
Heat-aware dispatch as efficiency-vs-equity optimization, analog of my EV-charging Pareto framing
